title: Priority queue applications

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Priority queue applications; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Priority queue applications
sources.claim: A priority queue exposes the highest- or lowest-priority item rather than arrival order and is commonly implemented with a heap.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A priority queue exposes the highest- or lowest-priority item rather than arrival order and is commonly implemented with a heap.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Dijkstra pushes an improved distance and ignores a popped stale pair whose distance no longer matches the best table. Your goal is to solve and analyze a priority-driven problem with the selected language's library or implementation. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Array representation and operations; Tree terminology and representation; Heapify, insert and extract. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: A priority queue exposes the highest- or lowest-priority item rather than arrival order and is commonly implemented with a heap. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: In C, tree nodes and heap buffers need explicit lifetime and bounds; C++ can combine RAII nodes with vector heaps; Java uses object references and PriorityQueue contracts; Python uses objects plus heapq's min-heap functions, while the conceptual invariant stays unchanged.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Equal-priority stability is not automatic, decrease-key support varies, and stale-entry techniques may be needed. The draft therefore rejects: A priority queue always dequeues equal-priority items in insertion order.

includes: Use prioritized removal for scheduling, top-k selection and graph frontiers with stable tie policy when required.
Solve and analyze a priority-driven problem with the selected language's library or implementation.

In C, tree nodes and heap buffers need explicit lifetime and bounds; C++ can combine RAII nodes with vector heaps; Java uses object references and PriorityQueue contracts; Python uses objects plus heapq's min-heap functions, while the conceptual invariant stays unchanged.
excludes: Equal-priority stability is not automatic, decrease-key support varies, and stale-entry techniques may be needed.

outcomes: Solve and analyze a priority-driven problem with the selected language's library or implementation.

examples.title: Priority queue applications: language-neutral trace
examples.situation: Dijkstra pushes an improved distance and ignores a popped stale pair whose distance no longer matches the best table.
walkthrough: Step 1 — State the abstract input and invariant: Dijkstra pushes an improved distance and ignores a popped stale pair whose distance no longer matches the best table.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Stability among equal priorities requires an explicit tie-breaker; ordinary heap priority alone does not guarantee it.
examples.result: The conceptual result follows because Stability among equal priorities requires an explicit tie-breaker; ordinary heap priority alone does not guarantee it.

Step 2 — Map storage, library, equality, bounds, ownership, and error rules using: In C, tree nodes and heap buffers need explicit lifetime and bounds; C++ can combine RAII nodes with vector heaps; Java uses object references and PriorityQueue contracts; Python uses objects plus heapq's min-heap functions, while the conceptual invariant stays unchanged.
Step 3 — Retest this boundary in the selected implementation: Equal-priority stability is not automatic, decrease-key support varies, and stale-entry techniques may be needed.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: A priority queue always dequeues equal-priority items in insertion order.
misconceptions.correction: Stability among equal priorities requires an explicit tie-breaker; ordinary heap priority alone does not guarantee it.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use an invariant or counterexample to explain why this DSA belief fails: A priority queue always dequeues equal-priority items in insertion order.
